title: One in a hundred
summary: On her final week, a Copenhagen tax inspector tells a junior colleague what she has actually learned about taxpayers in thirty-nine years — and why most of his training is preparing him for the wrong cases.

body.speaker: Mrs Larsen
body.text: Tobias. Sit. The coffee here is bad. I have always known this.

body.speaker: Tobias
body.text: Then why do you drink it?

body.speaker: Mrs Larsen
body.text: Because it is the office coffee. One does not need to enjoy everything one accepts.

body.speaker: Tobias
body.text: Are you really retiring on Friday?

body.speaker: Mrs Larsen
body.text: Friday at four. Thirty-nine years and eight months.

body.speaker: Tobias
body.text: I came to ask you something.

body.speaker: Mrs Larsen
body.text: Make it useful.

body.speaker: Tobias
body.text: When you started, did you think of taxpayers as people trying to break the rules?

body.speaker: Mrs Larsen
body.text: For the first six years. I was wrong about most of them. About ten per cent are.

body.speaker: Tobias
body.text: Only ten?

body.speaker: Mrs Larsen
body.text: Yes. And of that ten, most are not breaking rules deliberately. They are tired, confused, given bad advice, or trying to make a small business survive. About one in a hundred of the audits I have done involved a person who was actually trying to cheat the state and knew exactly what he was doing.

body.speaker: Tobias
body.text: One in a hundred.

body.speaker: Mrs Larsen
body.text: One in a hundred. The other ninety-nine were a different kind of case. They needed help reading their own books. Some of them needed help arguing with a bank. A few needed help understanding that they had been overpaying for years.

body.speaker: Tobias
body.text: Overpaying?

body.speaker: Mrs Larsen
body.text: It happens. Honest people are not always efficient people. A good inspector finds both directions of the error. I have, in my career, reduced more tax bills than I have increased.

body.speaker: Tobias
body.text: Is that allowed?

body.speaker: Mrs Larsen
body.text: It is required, Tobias. We are not collectors. We are the people who make sure the books are accurate. Sometimes accurate means lower.

body.speaker: Tobias
body.text: I had not thought of it that way.

body.speaker: Mrs Larsen
body.text: You will. Most of your training emphasises the cases that are sent to court. Those are the loud ones. The quiet majority of our work is uninteresting reconciliation, conducted in offices where the taxpayer cries once, briefly, when he realises the bill is real, and then asks how to pay it in instalments.

body.speaker: Tobias
body.text: What is the most important thing you have learned?

body.speaker: Mrs Larsen
body.text: That the person across the table from me is almost never my enemy. The state I work for is also not my enemy, but it is more often careless about the person than I am. My job, very specifically, is to be the person in the room who has read all the books, including the ones the state would rather forget.

body.speaker: Tobias
body.text: That sounds — political.

body.speaker: Mrs Larsen
body.text: It is administrative. The political version is what happens when no inspector is in the room.

annotations.meaningVi: kiểm toán / kiểm tra thuế
người nộp thuế
trả góp / trả thành nhiều đợt
đối chiếu / hoà giải sổ sách
thuộc hành chính / kỹ thuật điều hành
sổ sách kế toán / quy định pháp lý
vi phạm quy tắc / lách luật (cố ý)
được tư vấn sai / bị hướng dẫn không đúng — ngụ ý nạn nhân của bên thứ ba
giữ một doanh nghiệp nhỏ tồn tại — diễn đạt sự đấu tranh thực tế của doanh nhân
lừa dối / gian lận với nhà nước
một dạng vụ việc khác — dùng để phân loại hoặc tái phân khung vấn đề trong lập luận
tranh cãi / đàm phán với ngân hàng — hàm ý người dân đơn độc đối mặt tổ chức lớn
trả dư / nộp thừa (tiền, thuế)
cả hai chiều sai lệch — chỉ lỗi cả theo hướng tăng lẫn giảm; cụm trừu tượng quan trọng trong lập luận cân bằng

annotations.phrase: reduced more tax bills than I have increased
annotations.meaningVi: giảm nhiều hoá đơn thuế hơn là tăng — mẫu so sánh đối lập dùng để đảo ngược kỳ vọng của người nghe

annotations.phrase: make sure the books are accurate
annotations.meaningVi: đảm bảo sổ sách chính xác — diễn đạt trách nhiệm kiểm soát chất lượng

annotations.phrase: emphasises the cases that are sent to court
annotations.meaningVi: nhấn mạnh các vụ ra toà — dùng để chỉ ra sự thiên lệch trong đào tạo hoặc nhận thức
đa số yên lặng — cụm từ đối lập với thiểu số gây ồn ào; dùng để điều chỉnh nhận thức bị bóp méo
được tiến hành trong văn phòng — nhấn mạnh tính bình thường, không kịch tính của công việc hành chính
ở phía bên kia bàn — hình ảnh ẩn dụ chỉ người đối diện trong một cuộc đàm phán hoặc kiểm tra
thờ ơ / vô tâm với con người cụ thể — chỉ sự thiếu nhân văn của cơ cấu quan liêu

annotations.phrase: the ones the state would rather forget
annotations.meaningVi: những điều nhà nước muốn lãng quên — cụm ngụ ý thông tin bất tiện bị hệ thống né tránh; giọng phê phán nhẹ nhàng
rất cụ thể / một cách chính xác — trạng từ nhấn mạnh dùng để thu hẹp và làm rõ phạm vi tuyên bố
hầu như không bao giờ — hedging language: phủ định có điều kiện, tránh tuyệt đối hoá
thanh tra thuế, kiểm tra viên thuế (người có thẩm quyền kiểm tra hồ sơ thuế)

grammarNotes.title: Quantified generalisation: 'about X%', 'one in a hundred', 'most', 'the quiet majority'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi nói về xu hướng chung dựa trên kinh nghiệm dài, B2 dùng các cụm định lượng để vừa nói được điều khái quát vừa không quá tuyệt đối. Bài có: 'About ten per cent are.' 'About one in a hundred of the audits…' 'I have reduced more tax bills than I have increased.' 'The quiet majority of our work…' Cách diễn đạt này giúp người nói nghe có thẩm quyền kinh nghiệm mà vẫn để chỗ cho ngoại lệ — đặc biệt phù hợp với người làm công việc mà mỗi ca lại khác nhau.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: When generalising from long experience, B2 speakers use quantified hedges to claim a pattern without overstating. The passage offers: 'About ten per cent are.' 'About one in a hundred of the audits…' 'I have reduced more tax bills than I have increased.' 'The quiet majority of our work…' These phrasings sound authoritative without claiming certainty — ideal for jobs where every case differs.

translationVi:
Bà Larsen: Tobias. Ngồi đi. Cà phê chỗ này dở. Tôi luôn biết điều đó.
Tobias: Vậy sao bà vẫn uống?
Bà Larsen: Vì đó là cà phê văn phòng. Một người không cần thích mọi thứ mình chấp nhận.
Tobias: Thứ Sáu này bà nghỉ hưu thật ạ?
Bà Larsen: Thứ Sáu, bốn giờ. Ba mươi chín năm tám tháng.
Tobias: Cháu đến hỏi bà một câu.
Bà Larsen: Hỏi câu có ích vào.
Tobias: Khi bà bắt đầu, bà có nghĩ người nộp thuế là người đang cố lách luật không?
Bà Larsen: Sáu năm đầu thì có. Tôi đã sai về phần lớn họ. Khoảng mười phần trăm thì có.
Tobias: Chỉ mười?
Bà Larsen: Đúng. Trong mười đó, phần lớn không cố ý phá luật. Họ mệt, rối, được tư vấn tệ, hoặc đang cố giữ một doanh nghiệp nhỏ sống sót. Khoảng một trên một trăm vụ kiểm toán tôi từng làm liên quan đến một người thực sự cố lừa nhà nước và biết rõ mình đang làm gì.
Tobias: Một trên một trăm.
Bà Larsen: Một trên một trăm. Chín mươi chín vụ còn lại là loại khác. Họ cần giúp đọc chính sổ sách của họ. Vài người cần giúp tranh cãi với ngân hàng. Một số cần giúp để hiểu rằng nhiều năm qua họ nộp dư.
Tobias: Nộp dư?
Bà Larsen: Có. Người trung thực không phải lúc nào cũng hiệu quả. Một thanh tra giỏi tìm cả hai hướng sai. Trong sự nghiệp, tôi đã giảm nhiều hoá đơn thuế hơn là tăng.
Tobias: Vậy được phép sao?
Bà Larsen: Là bắt buộc, Tobias. Chúng ta không phải người đi đòi tiền. Chúng ta là người bảo đảm sổ sách chính xác. Đôi khi 'chính xác' nghĩa là 'thấp hơn'.
Tobias: Cháu chưa nghĩ theo cách đó.
Bà Larsen: Em sẽ. Phần lớn việc đào tạo của em nhấn mạnh các vụ ra toà. Đó là các vụ ồn. Đa số yên lặng của công việc chúng ta là đối chiếu sổ sách không thú vị, làm trong phòng — nơi người nộp thuế khóc một lần, ngắn thôi, lúc nhận ra hoá đơn là thật, rồi hỏi cách trả góp.
Tobias: Điều quan trọng nhất bà đã học được là gì?
Bà Larsen: Rằng người ngồi đối diện tôi gần như không bao giờ là kẻ thù của tôi. Nhà nước mà tôi làm cho cũng không phải kẻ thù của tôi, nhưng nó hay vô tâm hơn tôi với người ngồi đó. Việc của tôi, rất cụ thể, là người trong phòng đã đọc hết các sách, kể cả những sách nhà nước muốn quên.
Tobias: Nghe — chính trị.
Bà Larsen: Là hành chính. Phiên bản chính trị là khi không có thanh tra nào trong phòng.

questions.prompt: How long has Mrs Larsen worked as a tax inspector?
What did she think of taxpayers at the start?
options: That all of them were honest
That they were people trying to break the rules — for the first six years
questions.hint: Mrs Larsen's reply to the first question.

questions.prompt: What proportion of taxpayers are actually breaking rules, in her estimate?
Of that ten per cent, what are most actually doing?
options: Tired, confused, given bad advice, or trying to make a small business survive — not deliberately cheating
questions.explanation: 'Most are not breaking rules deliberately. They are tired, confused, given bad advice, or trying to make a small business survive.'

questions.prompt: How many of her audits involved someone who knew exactly what he was doing?
questions.explanation: 'About one in a hundred of the audits I have done involved a person who was actually trying to cheat the state and knew exactly what he was doing.'

questions.prompt: What did the other ninety-nine in a hundred actually need?
options: Help reading their own books; help arguing with a bank; help realising they had been overpaying for years
questions.explanation: Mrs Larsen lists exactly these three kinds of help.
questions.hint: Look at her longer reply about the ninety-nine.

questions.prompt: Why does she say 'honest people are not always efficient people'?
options: Honest taxpayers can make errors that cost them money, and a good inspector finds both directions of the error.
questions.explanation: 'A good inspector finds both directions of the error.'
questions.hint: Look at her explanation of overpayment.

questions.prompt: Has she more often reduced or increased tax bills in her career?
options: She has reduced more tax bills than she has increased.
questions.explanation: 'I have, in my career, reduced more tax bills than I have increased.'

questions.prompt: How does she describe the inspector's role?
options: Collectors of money for the state
People who make sure the books are accurate — sometimes accurate means lower.
Defenders of the state at any cost
questions.explanation: 'We are not collectors. We are the people who make sure the books are accurate. Sometimes accurate means lower.'

questions.prompt: What does she say Tobias's training emphasises?
options: The cases that are sent to court — the loud ones
questions.explanation: 'Most of your training emphasises the cases that are sent to court. Those are the loud ones.'

questions.prompt: How does she describe the quiet majority of the work?
options: Uninteresting reconciliation, conducted in offices where the taxpayer cries once, briefly, then asks how to pay in instalments
questions.explanation: Her description of the quiet majority is exactly this.
questions.hint: Look at the long sentence about the majority.

questions.prompt: What is the most important thing Mrs Larsen has learned?
options: That the person across the table is almost never her enemy; the state is not her enemy either but is more often careless about the person than she is — her job is to be the one who has read all the books, including the ones the state would rather forget.
That she should have become a judge.
questions.explanation: Mrs Larsen states this in her long final philosophical reply.
questions.hint: Look at her longest answer about the most important lesson.

questions.prompt: Tobias calls her view 'political.' How does she correct him?
options: She calls it administrative. The political version, she says, is what happens when no inspector is in the room.
She says all work is political.
questions.explanation: 'It is administrative. The political version is what happens when no inspector is in the room.'

questions.prompt: Which of these is the dialogue NOT claiming?
options: That most taxpayers are deliberately cheating the state.
That a good inspector finds errors in both directions, lowering as well as raising bills.
That training and court cases are not representative of the actual work.
That the state can be more careless about the person than the inspector is.
questions.explanation: Mrs Larsen explicitly says only about ten per cent are, and only one in a hundred deliberately so.
questions.hint: Look for the option that contradicts her statistics.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: Public-service work is mostly quiet, two-directional accuracy on behalf of ordinary people — a kind of administrative ethics that protects citizens from the state's carelessness as much as the state from citizens' errors.
Court cases are the only meaningful work.
questions.explanation: Mrs Larsen reframes inspection as a balance of two errors and a quiet defence of the person across the table.
questions.hint: Notice that she defends 'lower' as well as 'higher' bills.

cloze.template:
Mrs Larsen: Tobias. Sit. The {{b1}} here is bad. I have always known this.
Tobias: Then why do you drink it?
Mrs Larsen: Because it is the office coffee. One does not need to enjoy everything one accepts.
Tobias: Are you really retiring on Friday?
Mrs Larsen: Friday at four. Thirty-nine years and {{b2}} months.
Tobias: I came to ask you something.
Mrs Larsen: Make it useful.
Tobias: When you started, did you think of taxpayers as people trying to break the rules?
Mrs Larsen: For the first {{b3}} years. I was wrong about most of them. About ten per cent are.
Tobias: Only ten?
Mrs Larsen: Yes. And of that ten, most are not breaking rules deliberately. They are tired, {{b4}}, given bad advice, or trying to make a small business survive. About one in a hundred of the audits I have done involved a person who was actually trying to {{b5}} the state and knew exactly what he was doing.
Tobias: One in a hundred.
Mrs Larsen: One in a hundred. The other ninety-nine were a different kind of case. They needed help reading their own {{b6}}. Some of them needed help arguing with a bank. A few needed help understanding that they had been {{b7}} for years.
Tobias: Overpaying?
Mrs Larsen: It happens. Honest people are not always efficient people. A good inspector finds both directions of the {{b8}}. I have, in my career, reduced more tax bills than I have increased.
Tobias: Is that allowed?
Mrs Larsen: It is required, Tobias. We are not {{b9}}. We are the people who make sure the books are accurate. Sometimes accurate means lower.
Tobias: I had not thought of it that way.
Mrs Larsen: You will. Most of your training emphasises the cases that are sent to {{b10}}. Those are the loud ones. The quiet majority of our work is uninteresting reconciliation, conducted in offices where the taxpayer cries once, briefly, when he realises the bill is real, and then asks how to pay it in {{b11}}.
Tobias: What is the most important thing you have learned?
Mrs Larsen: That the person across the table from me is almost never my {{b12}}. The state I work for is also not my enemy, but it is more often {{b13}} about the person than I am. My job, very specifically, is to be the person in the room who has read all the books, including the ones the state would rather {{b14}}.
Tobias: That sounds — political.
Mrs Larsen: It is administrative. The political version is what happens when no inspector is in the {{b15}}.

blanks.explanation: Actually trying to cheat the state.
They had been overpaying for years.
More often careless about the person.
The books the state would rather forget.
When no inspector is in the room.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mrs Larsen says her real job is to be the person in the room who has read the books the state would rather forget — including the ones that lower the bill. Is there a role in your own work that you have framed as 'enforcing' one thing, but which could also be framed as 'protecting both sides from each other's errors'? What would change in your daily decisions if you reframed it?
